content.full_text:
Law Offices of

OLSON
CONNELLY
HAGEL
FONG &
LEIDIGH

October 16, 1992


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Assistant General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

RE: YES ON 167 - CALIFORNIANS FOR A RECOVERING
ECONOMY, SPONSORED BY THE CALIFORNIA TAX
REFORM ASSOCIATION

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I serve as treasurer for the above-referenced
Committee. On behalf of myself and the Committee, I am
requesting advice pursuant to Government Code section
§ 83114(b) with respect to our duties under the
campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform
Act.

LANCE H. OLSON
BRUCE J. HAGEL
LEROY Y. FONG
ROBERT E. LEIDIGH
GEORGE WATERS
DIANE M. FISHBURN

BACKGROUND

The Committee is primarily formed to support the
qualification and passage of Proposition 167 on the
November 3, 1992 statewide ballot.

Of Counsel
LLOYD G. CONNELLY

The Committee entered into a contract with
Interactive TeleMedia Services Corporation
("Interactive") which is in the business of providing
interactive telephone call processing services to its
customers in the conduct of 900 number marketing
programs. The contract between the Committee and
Interactive generally provides for the instituting of a
"900" telephone line service as a means by which
persons may contribute to the committee. A copy of the
contract is enclosed for your review and information.
The program began operating recently, and we are
approaching the first reporting cycle since the program
began.

Under the contract, the Committee provides the TV
commercial script and the telephone script.
Interactive establishes the 900 number line with the
telephone carrier, maintains the equipment, and
retrieves data from the callers.

CapitolBank Center
300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, CA 95814

Telephone [916] 442-2952
Facsimile [916] 442-1280

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
October 16, 1992
Page 2


     The 900 number program works as follows.  The TV viewer
views the ad and places a call to the 900 number.  The caller
listens to an 18-second message which states the phone charges
and provides an opportunity for the caller to hang up without
incurring any charges.  If the caller stays on the line, he or
she will listen to a short message about Proposition 167.
Callers may leave their name and address if they wish to make
further contributions.  The cost of the call is $5.00 for the
first minute, and $1.00 for each additional minute.

     The charges to the caller are billed on the caller's next
regular phone bill.  The telephone carrier collects the charges
with the payment of the telephone bill and forwards the money to
Interactive.  Interactive retains 10% of the "Gross Call
Proceeds" to cover charge backs and credits, which callers may
request or be entitled to.  In addition, Interactive will deduct
its fees and charges.  Then Interactive will forward the balance
of the proceeds to the Committee.  This payment will be made 75
days after the end of the month when the call occurred.
Accordingly, for calls made in October, the payment will be
received by the Committee in December.

     Interactive will provide to the Committee daily activity
reports on the daily call volume data for each 900 number.  In
addition, Interactive will provide to the Committee on a periodic
basis, the data derived from the transcription of the callers'
messages.

                              QUESTIONS

     1.   With respect to the telephone charges incurred by the
callers:

          a.   Are these charges contributions?

          b.   If the charges are contributions, what is the date
of the contribution for reporting purposes?

          c.   Since the average call will be 2 minutes, for a
total charge of $6.00, may the Committee assume for reporting and
recordkeeping purposes that the individual contributions are
under $25?

          d.   How should the total amount of the contributions
be calculated?  Should the amounts withheld by Interactive for
charge backs and credits be included?  Should the amounts
withheld by Interactive for its fees and charges be included?

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
October 16, 1992
Page 3

2.     With respect to the program expenditures:

a.     How should the retention of a percentage of the
proceeds by Interactive for charge backs and credits be reported
by the Committee? If these are reportable payments, what are the
dates of the payment?

b.     How should the Committee report the other
Interactive charges and fees which are retained by Interactive
out of the gross proceeds? If these are reportable payments,
what are the dates of the payments?

c.     Is there any required subvendor reporting in these
circumstances?  If yes, what type of reporting is required and
what types of efforts are required to obtain the subvendor
information?

3.     What records is the Committee required to keep in
connection with this program?

     Please contact me if you need any additional information.
Since the Committee is currently filing pre-election campaign
reports in connection with the November 3, 1992, we would
appreciate an expedited response to these questions.

     Thank you for your time and attention to this matter.

Very truly yours,

OLSON, CONNELLY, HAGEL, FONG & LEIDIGH

[signature]
LANCE H. OLSON

LHO:jlh
Enclosures

sections.question:
1. With respect to the telephone charges incurred by the callers:

a. Are these charges contributions?

b. If the charges are contributions, what is the date of the contribution for reporting purposes?

c. Since the average call will be 2 minutes, for a total charge of $6.00, may the Committee assume for reporting and recordkeeping purposes that the individual contributions are under $25?

d. How should the total amount of the contributions be calculated? Should the amounts withheld by Interactive for charge backs and credits be included? Should the amounts withheld by Interactive for its fees and charges be included?

2. With respect to the program expenditures:

    a. How should the retention of a percentage of the proceeds by Interactive for charge backs and credits be reported by the Committee? If these are reportable payments, what are the dates of the payment?

    b. How should the Committee report the other Interactive charges and fees which are retained by Interactive out of the gross proceeds? If these are reportable payments, what are the dates of the payments?

    c. Is there any required subvendor reporting in these circumstances? If yes, what type of reporting is required and what types of efforts are required to obtain the subvendor information?

3. What records is the Committee required to keep in connection with this program?

Please contact me if you need any additional information. Since the Committee is currently filing pre-election campaign reports in connection with the November 3, 1992, we would appreciate an expedited response to these questions.

Thank you for your time and attention to this matter.
sections.facts:
The Committee is primarily formed to support the qualification and passage of Proposition 167 on the November 3, 1992 statewide ballot.

The Committee entered into a contract with Interactive TeleMedia Services Corporation ("Interactive") which is in the business of providing interactive telephone call processing services to its customers in the conduct of 900 number marketing programs. The contract between the Committee and Interactive generally provides for the instituting of a "900" telephone line service as a means by which persons may contribute to the committee. A copy of the contract is enclosed for your review and information. The program began operating recently, and we are approaching the first reporting cycle since the program began.

Under the contract, the Committee provides the TV commercial script and the telephone script. Interactive establishes the 900 number line with the telephone carrier, maintains the equipment, and retrieves data from the callers.

The 900 number program works as follows. The TV viewer views the ad and places a call to the 900 number. The caller listens to an 18-second message which states the phone charges and provides an opportunity for the caller to hang up without incurring any charges. If the caller stays on the line, he or she will listen to a short message about Proposition 167. Callers may leave their name and address if they wish to make further contributions. The cost of the call is $5.00 for the first minute, and $1.00 for each additional minute.

The charges to the caller are billed on the caller's next regular phone bill. The telephone carrier collects the charges with the payment of the telephone bill and forwards the money to Interactive. Interactive retains 10% of the "Gross Call Proceeds" to cover charge backs and credits, which callers may request or be entitled to. In addition, Interactive will deduct its fees and charges. Then Interactive will forward the balance of the proceeds to the Committee. This payment will be made 75 days after the end of the month when the call occurred. Accordingly, for calls made in October, the payment will be received by the Committee in December.

Interactive will provide to the Committee daily activity reports on the daily call volume data for each 900 number. In addition, Interactive will provide to the Committee on a periodic basis, the data derived from the transcription of the callers' messages.

a. Are these charges contributions?

b. If the charges are contributions, what is the date of the contribution for reporting purposes?

c. Since the average call will be 2 minutes, for a total charge of $6.00, may the Committee assume for reporting and recordkeeping purposes that the individual contributions are under $25?

d. How should the total amount of the contributions be calculated? Should the amounts withheld by Interactive for charge backs and credits be included? Should the amounts withheld by Interactive for its fees and charges be included?

2. With respect to the program expenditures:

    a. How should the retention of a percentage of the proceeds by Interactive for charge backs and credits be reported by the Committee? If these are reportable payments, what are the dates of the payment?

    b. How should the Committee report the other Interactive charges and fees which are retained by Interactive out of the gross proceeds? If these are reportable payments, what are the dates of the payments?

    c. Is there any required subvendor reporting in these circumstances? If yes, what type of reporting is required and what types of efforts are required to obtain the subvendor information?

3. What records is the Committee required to keep in connection with this program?

Please contact me if you need any additional information. Since the Committee is currently filing pre-election campaign reports in connection with the November 3, 1992, we would appreciate an expedited response to these questions.

Thank you for your time and attention to this matter.
